body:
Japan regulates digital assets the way it regulates most things in its financial system: by repeatedly amending two existing statutes, not by legislating a dedicated crypto code. That structural fact -- amend, don't replace -- is the throughline of every pillar board on this site for this jurisdiction, and is worth holding in mind before reading any of them.

Who regulates what

The Financial Services Agency (FSA) is Japan's single integrated financial regulator -- one authority spanning banking, securities, insurance, and payments supervision, a contrast worth noting against fragmented, multi-agency regimes elsewhere. FSA registers and supervises crypto-asset exchange service providers and electronic-payment-instrument (stablecoin) service providers under the Payment Services Act, and financial instruments business operators dealing in security tokens under the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act; registration is processed in practice through Local Finance Bureaus, FSA's regional offices, rather than at FSA's Tokyo headquarters directly. Below FSA sit self-regulatory organisations it certifies rather than runs itself: the Japan Virtual and Crypto assets Exchange Association (JVCEA) is FSA's certified self-regulatory body for crypto-asset exchange business and, since May 2020, for crypto-asset derivatives trading and electronic-payment-instrument exchange business too, setting binding member rules on listings, new-token sales, advertising, and system governance on top of FSA's statutory registration regime; the Japan STO Association (JSTOA) plays the equivalent certified role for security-token dealing under the Financial Instruments and Exchange Act. Outside FSA's own perimeter, the Bank of Japan (BOJ) oversees payment systems and separately runs its own central bank digital currency Pilot Programme, exploratory work that has produced no decision to issue a digital yen. And the tax authorities run a distinct, currently live question of their own: how crypto-asset gains are taxed, addressed below.

The structural throughline

Japan built its entire digital-asset regime by amending two pre-existing statutes. The Payment Services Act (PSA) has been amended four times on point: it gained a crypto-asset exchange-business registration regime in April 2017 (following the 2014 Mt. Gox collapse); a hardened registration perimeter covering custody-only business, plus a rename from "virtual currency" to "crypto assets," in May 2020 (following the 2018 Coincheck incident); a dedicated stablecoin framework -- "electronic payment instruments" -- in June 2023; and, most recently, provisions creating a lighter intermediary-business registration tier and enabling domestic handling of foreign-issued stablecoins, in force since June 2026. The Financial Instruments and Exchange Act (FIEA) was amended once, in May 2020 -- the same single amending act that hardened the PSA that year -- to bring security tokens within its existing securities-disclosure and business-conduct framework as "electronically recorded transferable rights," and to bring crypto-asset derivatives dealing within its Type I financial instruments business licensing category. This pattern sits closer to Switzerland's amend-existing-law approach than to the single-anchor-statute pattern used in the EU or UK, but with a materially higher number of separate, crypto-specific amending acts layered onto the same two statutes over nearly a decade.

In force now, and what is still evolving

Everything described above is current law, not proposal. The one significant pending change is a bill that would migrate crypto-asset business regulation itself out of the PSA and into FIEA -- reclassifying crypto-assets as a financial product distinct from securities, creating a crypto-asset insider-trading prohibition and an administrative surcharge regime, and introducing new information-disclosure duties. The bill was submitted to the 221st ordinary Diet session on 10 April 2026, passed the House of Representatives on 11 June 2026, and was referred to the House of Councillors' Financial Affairs Committee on 15 June 2026; as of this page's writing (12 July 2026), the House of Councillors has recorded no committee vote and no plenary vote, so the bill remains pending, not enacted. A linked but legally separate tax-reform discussion -- a Cabinet-decided outline proposing to replace today's comprehensive-income tax treatment of crypto-asset gains (which reaches rates as high as roughly 55%) with a flat 20% separate-taxation rate -- is itself expressly conditioned by the government's own tax outline on the FIEA bill's enactment and entry into force, so neither change takes effect until the bill does.

The notable feature

Japan's June 2023 electronic-payment-instrument framework was among the earliest dedicated national stablecoin regimes anywhere, built years before comparable rules existed in most other major jurisdictions. Its defining design choice is on the issuer side: issuance of a digital-money-type stablecoin is restricted, by the structure of the statute's own definitions, to banks, registered fund-transfer service providers, and licensed trust companies -- entities already subject to deposit-taking, funds-transfer, or trust-law supervision, rather than to a newly created stablecoin-issuer licence category of its own.

A note on language

Japanese statutes, and most FSA publications, are authentic only in their Japanese original; where FSA or other regulators publish an English-language version, that page is the agency's own courtesy translation, not a second authentic text, and several of those translations lag years behind the current Japanese law. This radar's summary cards state which language a quoted source is in, and do not treat an English courtesy translation as equivalent to the Japanese original when the two might diverge.

How to use this site

The pillar boards give the current state of each of the site's seven regulatory areas for Japan in one place. The trajectory board lists officially announced dates and windows -- including the pending FIEA/PSA migration bill's Diet progress and the conditional tax-reform timeline above -- together. The document library indexes every primary-source document the watcher has observed. The glossary defines the jargon used throughout, and terms shared with other jurisdictions link to a single cross-jurisdiction entry. Every summary card cites the specific primary source it is drawn from.

AI-generated summary for general information. Not legal or regulatory advice. Always verify against the linked primary source.
